schools.summary: God calls Noah into the ark: 'and the LORD said unto Noah, Come thou and all thy house into the ark; for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation' — God inviting Noah and his whole household to enter the ark ('Come,' rather than 'go,' suggesting God is, as it were, in the ark, calling Noah into the refuge with him, the gracious call to enter the place of safety before the flood, the whole household to be preserved); 'all thy house' (Noah's family, his wife, sons, and sons' wives, the eight souls, 1 Peter 3:20, included in the salvation, God saving not Noah only but his house, the covenant grace extending to the family of the righteous); 'for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation' (God declaring Noah righteous before him amid a corrupt generation, Noah's righteousness, the fruit of grace and faith, 6:8-9; Hebrews 11:7, the reason given for his preservation, the one righteous man, found amid universal corruption, saved, God's regard to the righteous in the day of judgment, 2 Peter 2:5, 9) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry, Clarke.

themes: Come thou and all thy house into the ark; for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation
God's command to Noah to enter the ark, with his house
the righteous man and his household preserved amid the corrupt world
Noah's righteousness the ground of his preservation

schools.summary: The clean and unclean beasts: 'of every clean beast thou shalt take to thee by sevens, the male and his female: and of beasts that are not clean by two, the male and his female' — Noah to take seven (or seven pairs) of every clean beast (more of the clean animals preserved than of the unclean, which are by twos, 6:19, the greater number of clean beasts providing for sacrifice, 8:20, and for man's later use); the distinction of 'clean' and 'not clean' beasts existing before the Mosaic law (which later codified it, Leviticus 11, the distinction, known to Noah, probably for sacrifice, the clean being the animals fit for offering to God, the early knowledge of clean and unclean, given by God or by tradition from the beginning); 'of beasts that are not clean by two, the male and his female' (the unclean beasts preserved by pairs, a male and female of each, sufficient to continue the kind, fewer of the unclean, since they were not needed for sacrifice, the preservation of all the kinds, clean and unclean, but more of the clean) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill, Clarke.

themes: Of every clean beast thou shalt take to thee by sevens... and of beasts that are not clean by two, the male and his female
the distinction of clean and unclean beasts in the gathering
more of the clean preserved, for sacrifice and use
the distinction known before the Mosaic law

schools.summary: The fowls preserved: 'of fowls also of the air by sevens, the male and the female; to keep seed alive upon the face of all the earth' — the clean fowls (and perhaps the birds generally) to be taken by sevens, male and female (the birds, like the beasts, preserved in the ark, the comprehensive gathering of the air-breathing creatures); the fowls preserved by sevens (the clean), male and female (the greater number providing for sacrifice and for the restocking of the earth, the careful preservation of the bird-kinds); 'to keep seed alive upon the face of all the earth' (the animals and birds preserved to keep their kinds, seed, alive, that the earth might be restocked after the flood, God's purpose in the preservation the renewal of the animal creation, the ark holding the seed of all the kinds, that they might replenish the earth, the divine care for the continuance of the creation) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: Of fowls also of the air by sevens, the male and the female; to keep seed alive upon the face of all the earth
the fowls preserved and the seed kept alive
the bird-kinds preserved to restock the earth after the flood
God's care for the continuance of the creation

schools.summary: The seven days and forty days: 'for yet seven days, and I will cause it to rain upon the earth forty days and forty nights; and every living substance that I have made will I destroy from off the face of the earth' — God giving Noah seven days' notice before the flood (the seven days a final respite, the last of God's longsuffering, during which Noah and the animals enter the ark, the brief, final delay before the judgment falls); the rain to fall 'forty days and forty nights' (the continuous, heavy rain, with the fountains of the deep, v.11, that would cause the flood, forty days and nights of unceasing downpour, the great deluge that would cover the earth); 'every living substance that I have made will I destroy from off the face of the earth' (all living things, save those in the ark, to be destroyed, God repeating the sentence of universal destruction, 6:7, 17, the comprehensive blotting out of all that lived on the earth, the judgment sure and total) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Wesley.

themes: For yet seven days, and I will cause it to rain upon the earth forty days and forty nights; and every living substance that I have made will I destroy
the seven days' warning and the forty days' rain
the final respite and the coming deluge
the sure, total destruction of all that lived

schools.summary: Noah's obedience: 'and Noah did according unto all that the LORD commanded him' — Noah obeying God in all that he was commanded (as 6:22, entering the ark with his family, gathering the animals, clean and unclean, and the food, in obedience to God, Noah's faith showing itself in complete obedience, Hebrews 11:7); 'according unto all' (his obedience entire and exact, he doing everything as God directed, holding back nothing, the full, careful obedience of faith, Noah the pattern of thorough, unquestioning obedience to God's word); Noah's obedience flowing from his faith (believing God's word concerning the flood, a thing not seen, Hebrews 11:7, he prepared the ark and obeyed, his obedience, against the scorn of the world and the seeming improbability of the flood, the active faith of the righteous, the model of the believer who obeys God's word and prepares for what God has warned) — Gill, Henry, JFB, Calvin.

themes: And Noah did according unto all that the LORD commanded him
Noah's obedience in all things
the entire, exact obedience flowing from faith
the model of the believer who obeys God's word

schools.summary: Noah's age at the flood: 'and Noah was six hundred years old when the flood of waters was upon the earth' — Noah's age, six hundred years, dating the flood (the flood coming in the six hundredth year of Noah's life, v.11 giving the day, the careful chronology marking the flood as a real, dated event in history); Noah six hundred years old at the flood (he living three hundred fifty years after, 9:28, to nine hundred fifty years, 9:29, the long-lived patriarch witnessing the old world's destruction and the new world's beginning, his age anchoring the chronology); 'the flood (mabbul) of waters was upon the earth' (the great deluge coming upon the earth, the flood, long announced and prepared for, now arriving, the dating by Noah's age marking the certainty and historicity of the event, the flood set in the framework of real time) — Gill, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: Noah was six hundred years old when the flood of waters was upon the earth
Noah's age at the flood
the flood dated by the patriarch's age, set in real time
the great deluge arriving as God had announced

schools.summary: Noah and his household enter: 'and Noah went in, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives with him, into the ark, because of the waters of the flood' — Noah and his whole household (the eight souls, 1 Peter 3:20) entering the ark, as God had commanded (v.1, they going in 'because of the waters of the flood,' in obedience and faith, taking refuge in the ark from the coming flood); the eight persons — Noah, his three sons, his wife, and his sons' wives — entering the ark (the family of the righteous preserved together, the household of faith taking refuge); 'because of the waters of the flood' (they entering in view of the coming flood, believing God's word, faith moving them to take refuge in the ark before the waters came, the obedience of faith acting on God's warning, Hebrews 11:7, 'moved with fear, prepared an ark,' the household saved by entering the refuge in faith) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: Noah went in, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives with him, into the ark, because of the waters of the flood
Noah and his household entering the ark
the eight souls entering the refuge in obedience and faith
the household saved by entering the ark before the waters came

schools.summary: The classes of creatures: 'of clean beasts, and of beasts that are not clean, and of fowls, and of every thing that creepeth upon the earth' — the verse listing the classes of creatures that entered the ark: clean beasts, unclean beasts, fowls, and creeping things (the comprehensive gathering of all the kinds of land-dwelling, air-breathing creatures, the animals preserved in their classes); both clean and unclean beasts gathered (the clean by sevens, the unclean by twos, vv.2-3, the distinction maintained, both classes preserved, that the kinds might survive); 'fowls, and... every thing that creepeth upon the earth' (the birds and the creeping things, with the beasts, making up the full range of the creatures preserved, all the kinds of air-breathing land creatures entering the ark, the comprehensive preservation of the animal creation, that it might be renewed after the flood) — Gill, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: Of clean beasts, and of beasts that are not clean, and of fowls, and of every thing that creepeth upon the earth
the classes of creatures gathered to the ark
the comprehensive preservation of all the animal kinds
both clean and unclean preserved that the kinds might survive

schools.summary: The creatures enter two by two: 'there went in two and two unto Noah into the ark, the male and the female, as God had commanded Noah' — the creatures entering the ark two by two (a male and female of each kind, as God had commanded, the basic pairs, with the additional clean by sevens, v.2, ensuring the continuance of every kind, the animals coming to Noah and entering the ark); the creatures 'went in... unto Noah' (they coming to Noah and entering the ark, God's providence, 6:20, bringing the animals to the ark, by instinct and divine direction, the creatures coming of themselves, not all hunted out by Noah, God's hand in gathering the kinds); 'the male and the female, as God had commanded Noah' (male and female of each kind, to propagate the species, the entrance of the pairs as God had commanded, the careful fulfillment of God's instruction, the breeding pairs preserved to renew the kinds after the flood) — Gill.

themes: There went in two and two unto Noah into the ark, the male and the female, as God had commanded Noah
the creatures entering the ark two by two
the breeding pairs brought to the ark by God's providence
the pairs preserved to renew the kinds after the flood

schools.summary: The flood comes after seven days: 'and it came to pass after seven days, that the waters of the flood were upon the earth' — after the seven days' respite (v.4), the flood coming (God's word exactly fulfilled, the waters of the flood coming upon the earth at the appointed time, the certainty and punctuality of God's judgment); the flood coming 'after seven days' (exactly as God had said, v.4, the seven days of final respite ending, and the flood beginning, God's word sure, fulfilled to the day, the appointed time of judgment come); 'the waters of the flood were upon the earth' (the great deluge beginning, the fountains of the deep and the windows of heaven, v.11, the flood, long announced, now coming, the judgment falling upon the corrupt world, the waters that would destroy all flesh coming upon the earth at God's appointed time) — Gill, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: it came to pass after seven days, that the waters of the flood were upon the earth
the coming of the flood after the seven days
the punctual fulfillment of God's word, the flood coming as he said
the judgment falling on the corrupt world at the appointed time

schools.summary: The sources of the flood: 'in the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, the seventeenth day of the month, the same day were all the fountains of the great deep broken up, and the windows of heaven were opened' — the exact dating, 'in the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, the seventeenth day of the month' (the flood dated to the day, the precise chronology marking the flood as a real, historical event, the day the judgment began recorded); 'all the fountains of the great deep (tehom) were broken up' (the subterranean waters, the great deep, bursting forth, the waters under the earth released, flooding up from below, the breaking up of the fountains of the deep one great source of the flood); 'the windows (floodgates) of heaven were opened' (the waters above, the atmospheric/celestial waters, pouring down, the heavens opening as floodgates, sending the forty days' rain, v.12, the two sources of the flood — the deep below and the heavens above — together causing the deluge, the figure of the ordered separation of waters, 1:6-7, undone, the waters above and below loosed upon the earth in judgment) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Henry, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: the same day were all the fountains of the great deep broken up, and the windows of heaven were opened
the breaking up of the deep and the opening of the windows of heaven
the waters below and above loosed upon the earth in judgment
the flood dated to the day, a real historical event

schools.summary: The forty days' rain: 'and the rain was upon the earth forty days and forty nights' — the rain (from the opened windows of heaven, v.11) falling continuously for forty days and forty nights (the unceasing, torrential rain, with the fountains of the deep, causing the flood to rise, the great deluge, exactly as God had said, v.4); the rain continuing 'forty days and forty nights' (a long, continuous downpour, the forty days marking the period of the flood's rising, the rain not ceasing until the waters had covered the earth, the relentless deluge); the forty days' rain fulfilling God's word (v.4, the deluge, long announced, falling upon the earth, the rain from above and the deep from below together flooding the world, the judgment proceeding exactly as God had warned) — Gill, Clarke, Wesley.

themes: And the rain was upon the earth forty days and forty nights
the forty days' and nights' rain
the continuous, torrential downpour causing the flood to rise
the deluge fulfilling God's word exactly

schools.summary: The household enters on the day of the flood: 'in the selfsame day entered Noah, and Shem, and Ham, and Japheth, the sons of Noah, and Noah's wife, and the three wives of his sons with them, into the ark' — on the very day the flood began (v.11), Noah and his household entering the ark (the entrance exactly timed to the coming of the flood, God bringing them into the refuge on the day of judgment, the precise providence of God in their preservation); the eight persons — Noah, his three sons, his wife, and his sons' wives (1 Peter 3:20) — entering the ark together (the whole household of the righteous preserved, the named family, the remnant saved from the flood, through whom the race would be renewed); 'with them, into the ark' (the family entering the ark together, the place of safety, God's grace gathering the household into the refuge on the day of the flood, the eight souls, by faith and obedience, taking shelter in the ark from the waters, the salvation of the righteous remnant) — Ellicott, Henry, Calvin, Gill.

themes: In the selfsame day entered Noah... and Noah's wife, and the three wives of his sons with them, into the ark
Noah and his household entering the ark on the day of the flood
the eight souls gathered into the refuge by God's providence
saved on the day of judgment

schools.summary: All the kinds enter with Noah: 'they, and every beast after his kind, and all the cattle after their kind, and every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth after his kind, and every fowl after his kind, every bird of every sort' — with Noah's household entering all the kinds of creatures: beasts, cattle, creeping things, and fowls, every bird of every sort (the comprehensive gathering of all the air-breathing land creatures, each after its kind, into the ark); the repeated 'after his/their kind' (each species distinct, entering by kind, the preservation of the distinct kinds, the species of ch. 1, the ark holding representatives of all the kinds, that they might survive and be renewed); 'every bird of every sort (every winged creature)' (the comprehensive preservation of all the kinds of birds and winged creatures, nothing of the air-breathing land creation left to perish without preservation in the ark, the full range of the kinds gathered, God's care for the whole creation) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: every beast after his kind, and all the cattle after their kind, and every creeping thing... and every fowl after his kind, every bird of every sort
all the kinds of creatures entering the ark with Noah
the comprehensive preservation of all the distinct kinds
God's care for the whole creation

schools.summary: The creatures enter by pairs: 'and they went in unto Noah into the ark, two and two of all flesh, wherein is the breath of life' — the creatures entering the ark by twos (the basic pairs, with the clean by sevens, v.2, all flesh that had the breath of life, the air-breathing land creatures entering to be preserved, the pairs of all the kinds gathered); the creatures going in 'two and two' (male and female of each kind, to continue the species, the pairs preserving the kinds, the comprehensive entry of the breeding pairs of all flesh); 'wherein is the breath of life' (all the creatures that breathe, the air-breathing land animals and birds, the breath of life marking the living creatures preserved, those that breathed gathered into the ark, while those outside, with the breath of life, would perish, vv.21-22, the preservation of the breathing kinds) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: they went in unto Noah into the ark, two and two of all flesh, wherein is the breath of life
the creatures entering by pairs, all that had the breath of life
the breeding pairs of all the breathing creatures gathered into the ark
preserved while those outside would perish

schools.summary: The LORD shut him in: 'and they that went in, went in male and female of all flesh, as God had commanded him: and the LORD shut him in' — the creatures entering male and female, as God had commanded (the entry exactly as God directed, the breeding pairs of all flesh gathered, the careful fulfillment of God's instruction in the preservation of the kinds, all done as God had commanded, the gathering and entry of the creatures fulfilling God's command exactly, Noah's obedience and God's providence together accomplishing the preservation); 'the LORD shut him in' (God himself closing the door of the ark, the single door, 6:16, shut by God's own hand, the LORD sealing Noah and his household safely within, and shutting out the world, the figure of God's protecting care — Noah secured within — and of the closing of the door of mercy — the world shut out — after God shut the door, none could enter or leave, the ark, sealed by God, bearing Noah safely through the flood) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: they that went in, went in male and female of all flesh, as God had commanded him: and the LORD shut him in
the creatures entering male and female, and the LORD shutting Noah in
God himself sealing the door, securing Noah and shutting out the world
God's protecting care and the closing of the door of mercy

schools.summary: The rising flood lifts the ark: 'and the flood was forty days upon the earth; and the waters increased, and bare up the ark, and it was lift up above the earth' — during the forty days (the rain and the deep, vv.11-12), the waters rising and increasing (the flood growing, covering more and more of the earth, the waters steadily mounting as the deluge continued); the increasing waters bearing up the ark, and it being lift up above the earth (the rising waters lifting the ark and floating it above the earth, the ark, which had stood on the ground, borne up by the flood, the very waters of judgment, which destroyed the world, lifting and preserving the ark and those within, the ark floating safely on the deluge); the ark, borne up by the waters, preserving Noah and his household above the flood (the same flood that drowned the world carrying the ark and the righteous safely, God's preservation of his own amid the judgment, the ark, lifted above the earth, the refuge that rode out the deluge) — Gill, Henry, JFB, Calvin.

themes: the flood was forty days upon the earth; and the waters increased, and bare up the ark, and it was lift up above the earth
the rising flood lifting up the ark
the waters of judgment lifting and preserving the ark and the righteous
the refuge riding safely on the deluge

schools.summary: The waters prevail: 'and the waters prevailed, and were increased greatly upon the earth; and the ark went upon the face of the waters' — the waters growing mighty and increasing greatly, overwhelming the earth (the flood prevailing, rising higher and spreading wider, the deluge mounting to a great height, covering the land, the irresistible increase of the waters); the waters 'prevailed' (becoming mighty and dominant, overwhelming all, the flood now in full force, covering the earth, the waters' increasing prevalence marking the progress of the destruction); 'the ark went (floated) upon the face of the waters' (the ark riding safely upon the surface of the prevailing flood, borne up by the very waters that destroyed the world, the ark floating above them, Noah and his household, in the ark, carried safely on the face of the deluge, the refuge of God's preservation riding upon the waters of judgment) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: the waters prevailed, and were increased greatly upon the earth; and the ark went upon the face of the waters
the waters prevailing and the ark riding upon them
the deluge overwhelming the earth while the ark rode safely above
the refuge borne up by the waters of judgment

schools.summary: Gill and the traditional reading (the universal/global flood): 'and the waters prevailed exceedingly upon the earth; and all the high hills, that were under the whole heaven, were covered' — the waters growing exceedingly mighty, rising to a great height, covering the whole face of the earth; 'all the high hills, that were under the whole heaven, were covered' teaching that the whole earth, even the highest mountains, was submerged (the flood covering the entire globe, the universal extent of the deluge, as the universal corruption, 6:12, called for a universal judgment); Clarke defends the universality of the deluge against objectors; 'under the whole heaven' marking the universal extent (the whole earth under the whole sky), the universality of the language the chief support of the global reading.

The local-flood view: the flood covered the known world or the region inhabited by man — the 'high hills under the whole heaven' being the mountains of the region (or the whole visible horizon), the flood vast but not strictly global; Ellicott notes considerations bearing on the height (the fifteen cubits, v.20, perhaps above the mountains of the region); 'under the whole heaven' read as the whole region under the visible heaven, the universal language taken as the language of appearance or of the inhabited world. The extent of the flood divides between the universal/global reading (the whole earth and the highest mountains, the traditional view) and the local/regional reading.

debates.question: What was the extent of the flood — universal (global) or local (regional)?

sides.position: Universal/global — the flood covered the whole earth, even the highest mountains under the whole heaven (the traditional reading)
Local/regional — a vast flood over the known world or the region inhabited by man, the 'high hills' those of the region

themes: the waters prevailed exceedingly upon the earth; and all the high hills, that were under the whole heaven, were covered
the waters covering all the high hills (the crux of the flood's extent)
a universal/global flood vs a local/regional flood
the universality of the language the support of the global reading

schools.summary: The waters fifteen cubits above the mountains: 'fifteen cubits upward did the waters prevail; and the mountains were covered' — the waters rising fifteen cubits (about twenty-two feet) above the highest point (the depth sufficient to cover the mountains, fifteen cubits enough to float the ark over the highest peaks and to drown all that remained, the measure of the flood's prevailing height); fifteen cubits of water 'enough to drown' all the creatures (the depth above the mountains ensuring the total destruction, the waters rising high enough that nothing could survive on the highest ground, the ark, thirty cubits high, drawing about fifteen, floating clear above the mountains); 'the mountains were covered' (the waters covering the mountains, whether all the mountains of the globe, on the universal view, or the mountains of the region, on the local view, v.19, the fifteen cubits above the highest point ensuring the covering, the flood prevailing over the highest land, the total submersion of the earth or region marking the completeness of the judgment) — Clarke, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Fifteen cubits upward did the waters prevail; and the mountains were covered
the waters prevailing fifteen cubits above the mountains
the depth sufficient to drown all and float the ark clear
the total submersion marking the completeness of the judgment

schools.summary: The death of all flesh: 'and all flesh died that moved upon the earth, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of beast, and of every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth, and every man' — all living creatures on the earth (save those in the ark) dying in the flood (the comprehensive death of fowls, cattle, beasts, creeping things, and men, the universal destruction announced, 6:7, 17, accomplished, all flesh perishing); all the classes of air-breathing land creatures perishing (birds, cattle, wild beasts, and creeping things, the whole animal creation outside the ark swept away, the creatures, made for man, sharing in his judgment); 'and every man' (all mankind, save the eight in the ark, perishing in the flood, the corrupt race, given over to wickedness and violence, 6:5, 11, destroyed, the universal judgment falling on all men, the whole world of the ungodly perishing, as Peter notes, 2 Peter 2:5, 'spared not the old world... bringing in the flood upon the world of the ungodly,' the wages of sin paid to the corrupt race) — Gill, Henry, Wesley.

themes: all flesh died that moved upon the earth, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of beast, and of every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth, and every man
the death of all flesh in the flood
the universal destruction of all that moved on the earth save those in the ark
the corrupt world of the ungodly swept away

schools.summary: The death of all that breathed: 'all in whose nostrils was the breath of life, of all that was in the dry land, died' — every creature that breathed (had the breath of life in its nostrils), of all that lived on the dry land, dying in the flood (the comprehensive death of all air-breathing creatures outside the ark, the breath of life, given by God, 2:7, taken from all the living of the land); 'all in whose nostrils was the breath of life' (the air-breathing creatures, men and land animals, the qualification marking those that perished — the breathing creatures of the dry land, the fish and sea-creatures, breathing in the water, not included in the destruction); 'of all that was in the dry land, died' (all the land-dwelling, air-breathing creatures perishing, the flood destroying the inhabitants of the dry land, only those in the ark surviving, the total death of the breathing creatures of the land marking the completeness of the judgment) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: All in whose nostrils was the breath of life, of all that was in the dry land, died
the death of all that breathed on the dry land
every air-breathing creature of the land perishing
the breath of life taken from all save those in the ark

schools.summary: All destroyed, Noah only remaining: 'and every living substance was destroyed which was upon the face of the ground, both man, and cattle, and the creeping things, and the fowl of the heaven; and they were destroyed from the earth: and Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark' — every living thing on the face of the ground blotted out (the comprehensive destruction of man, cattle, creeping things, and birds, the whole order of land life, save the ark, swept away, the universal judgment completed); 'they were destroyed (blotted out) from the earth' (the living creatures removed from the earth, the destruction total and final, the corrupt world of the living blotted out, as God had purposed, 6:7, 'I will destroy... from the face of the earth'); 'Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark' (only Noah and those with him in the ark, the eight souls and the animals, surviving, the sole remnant preserved from the universal destruction, 'Noah only' marking the singular preservation of the righteous, the ark, with its few, the sole surviving life on the earth, from this remnant the world to be renewed, the saved remnant amid the universal judgment, 2 Peter 2:5) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB.

themes: every living substance was destroyed which was upon the face of the ground... and Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark
the destruction of all living things and Noah alone remaining
the universal destruction of all land life, the corrupt world blotted out
the sole surviving remnant in the ark, from whom the world would be renewed

schools.summary: The waters prevail 150 days: 'and the waters prevailed upon the earth an hundred and fifty days' — the waters prevailing (remaining at their height, or continuing mighty) upon the earth for a hundred and fifty days (the flood, after the forty days' rain, continuing to prevail for the full period, the long duration of the flood, the waters covering the earth for five months); the hundred and fifty days (including the forty days of rain, or reckoned from the beginning) marking the period during which the waters prevailed at their full height before they began to abate (8:3, the long months during which the earth lay under the flood, the duration marking the thoroughness of the judgment and the trial of Noah's faith and patience in the ark); through the hundred and fifty days, Noah and his household, and the creatures, preserved in the ark, borne up on the prevailing waters (God's care sustaining the remnant through the long flood, the waters prevailing but the ark and its few surviving, the flood running its appointed course before God remembered Noah and made the waters abate, 8:1) — Gill, Ellicott, Clarke.

themes: And the waters prevailed upon the earth an hundred and fifty days
the waters prevailing a hundred and fifty days
the deluge continuing at its height for five months
the ark and its few preserved through the long flood
